is a [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") record company and label founded in 1949 by [Bob Weinstock](/wiki/Bob_Weinstock "Bob Weinstock") in New York City which issued recordings in the mainstream, bop, and cool jazz idioms.[[2]](#cite_note-New_Grove-2) The company recorded hundreds of albums by many of the leading jazz musicians of the day, sometimes issuing them on subsidiary labels. The company's began releasing jazz records in 78 and 45 RPM formats in 1950. The Prestige label includes the 13000 and 25000 cat# series. Prestige International was a sub-label of Prestige, active from 1960 to 1969, that mostly released folk music. In 1971, the company was sold to [Fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy_Records "Fantasy Records"), which was later absorbed by [Concord](/wiki/Concord_Records "Concord Records").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American jazz record label

Not to be confused with the namesake Prestige Records, a British label that releases the work of [Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan_(singer) "Imran Khan (singer)").

Record label

| Prestige Records | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Parent company | [Concord](/wiki/Concord_(entertainment_company) "Concord (entertainment company)") |
| Founded | 1949; 77 years ago (1949)[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Founder | [Bob Weinstock](/wiki/Bob_Weinstock "Bob Weinstock") |
| Genre | [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") |
| Country of origin | United States |
| Location | [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") |
| Official website | [concordmusicgroup.com/labels/Prestige](http://concordmusicgroup.com/labels/Prestige) |

**Prestige Records** is a [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") record company and label founded in 1949 by [Bob Weinstock](/wiki/Bob_Weinstock "Bob Weinstock") in New York City which issued recordings in the mainstream, bop, and cool jazz idioms.[[2]](#cite_note-New_Grove-2) The company recorded hundreds of albums by many of the leading jazz musicians of the day, sometimes issuing them on subsidiary labels. The company's began releasing jazz records in 78 and 45 RPM formats in 1950. The Prestige label includes the 13000 and 25000 cat# series. Prestige International was a sub-label of Prestige, active from 1960 to 1969, that mostly released folk music. In 1971, the company was sold to [Fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy_Records "Fantasy Records"), which was later absorbed by [Concord](/wiki/Concord_Records "Concord Records").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prestige_Records&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Prestige office was located at 446 West 50th Street, [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Its catalogue included [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons"), [John Coltrane](/wiki/John_Coltrane "John Coltrane"), [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis"), [Stan Getz](/wiki/Stan_Getz "Stan Getz"), [Wardell Gray](/wiki/Wardell_Gray "Wardell Gray"), [Thelonious Monk](/wiki/Thelonious_Monk "Thelonious Monk"), and [Sonny Rollins](/wiki/Sonny_Rollins "Sonny Rollins").[[2]](#cite_note-New_Grove-2)

Audio engineer [Rudy Van Gelder](/wiki/Rudy_Van_Gelder "Rudy Van Gelder") was the recording engineer of many Prestige albums in the 1950s and early-to-mid-1960s. Prestige created new labels in 1960: Swingville, Moodsville, covering jazz, [Bluesville](/wiki/Bluesville_Records "Bluesville Records") featuring blues revival artists, Lively Arts featuring spoken word recordings and Prestige International, Prestige Folklore, Irish and Near East with folk and world music.[[2]](#cite_note-New_Grove-2)

By the later 1950s, Weinstock ceased supervising recording sessions directly, employing [Chris Albertson](/wiki/Chris_Albertson "Chris Albertson"), [Ozzie Cadena](/wiki/Ozzie_Cadena "Ozzie Cadena"), [Esmond Edwards](/wiki/Esmond_Edwards "Esmond Edwards"), [Don Schlitten](/wiki/Don_Schlitten "Don Schlitten"), and producer/music supervisor [Bob Porter](/wiki/Bob_Porter_(record_producer) "Bob Porter (record producer)"), among others, to fulfill this function.[[2]](#cite_note-New_Grove-2) Musicians recording for the label in the 1960s included [Jaki Byard](/wiki/Jaki_Byard "Jaki Byard") and [Booker Ervin](/wiki/Booker_Ervin "Booker Ervin"), while Prestige remained commercially viable by recording a number of [soul jazz](/wiki/Soul_jazz "Soul jazz") artists like [Charles Earland](/wiki/Charles_Earland "Charles Earland"). In 1966 the company's headquarters were located at 203 South Washington Avenue in [Bergenfield, New Jersey](/wiki/Bergenfield,_New_Jersey "Bergenfield, New Jersey").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The company was sold[[5]](#cite_note-label-5) to [Fantasy Records](/wiki/Fantasy_Records "Fantasy Records") in 1971, and original releases on the label formed a significant proportion of its [Original Jazz Classics](/wiki/Original_Jazz_Classics "Original Jazz Classics") line. Fantasy was purchased by [Concord Records](/wiki/Concord_Records "Concord Records") in 2005.[[5]](#cite_note-label-5)

In 2017, Concord Music Group revived the Prestige label. The first album released under the label's reactivation was *A Social Call* from Texas native [Jazzmeia Horn](/wiki/Jazzmeia_Horn "Jazzmeia Horn").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prestige_Records&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Discography")]

Main article: [Prestige Records discography](/wiki/Prestige_Records_discography "Prestige Records discography")
